issue is discussed briefly in the following comment

https://discourse.julialang.org/t/julia-threads-vs-blas-threads/8914/14?u=jlapeyre

The OP on the same thread mentions the issue.

The timings have improved since the post, but the basic problem remains:
```
julia> @btime BLAS.set_num_threads(3)
  1.243 μs (2 allocations: 32 bytes)

julia> my_BLAS_set_num_threads(n) =
          ccall((:openblas_set_num_threads64_, Base.libblas_name), Cvoid, (Int32,), n);

julia> @btime my_BLAS_set_num_threads(3)
  4.898 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

I had not used juliaup for a long time. `juliaup` is missing. But `~/bin/julialauncher` and configuration files and julia installations are present. I imagine this is not super uncommon. So `~/bin/julialauncher` exists and will start julia, but `juliaup` is not present.

The installation instructions say I should use curl to install the installer. I do this and the installer says it has found an existing configuration file. The only option it offers is to overwrite it. So I say "yes". It then ignores the existing julia installations and instead downloads and installs the latest stable version. It then fails to make the symlink to `~/.juliaup/julialauncher` because it is already there.  Because it fails, the next step, adding julialauncher to the path, does not happen. It turns out my installation is fine, other than this last fact. Because even thought it could not make the symlink, the symlink is already correct. I am not sure what modifications it planned to make to my path. So I give the curl command again. The installer is downloaded, and juliaup is downloaded again (even though it is already there). The latest configuration file is there, of course, but it is generated again. Then the installer downloads the latest stable version of julia and installs it over the existing installation that it made a few minutes earlier. I actually forgot to delete the symlink. So it fails again. I have to download the installer, juliaup, and the latest stable julia a third time. Now instructions for updating my `PATH` variable are printed. I have a working installation.

I think that the installer could do a better job of analyzing the existing juliaup installion. Both to be more informative to the user and to better recover from errors.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42080","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42080/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42080/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42080/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080","id":985383893,"node_id":"MDExOlB1bGxSZXF1ZXN0NzI0Njg2NTQw","number":42080,"title":"recommend against `using Foo` in package code (or "using considered harmful")","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":115619,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU2MTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/doc","name":"doc","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":"This change adds or pertains to documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":67,"created_at":"2021-09-01T17:00:06Z","updated_at":"2023-10-02T17:30:35Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42080","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I feel we are heading up against a "`using` crisis" where any new feature that is implemented by exporting a new name (either in Base or a package) becomes a breaking change. This is already happening (https://github.com/JuliaGPU/CUDA.jl/pull/1097, https://github.com/JuliaWeb/HTTP.jl/pull/745) and as projects get bigger and more names are exported, the likelihood of this rapidly increases.

The flaw in `using Foo` is fundamental in that you cannot lexically see where a name comes from so when two packages export the same name, you are screwed. Any code that relies on `using Foo` and then using an exported name from `Foo` is vulnerable to another dependency exporting the same name.
Therefore, I think we should start to strongly discourage the use of `using Foo` and only recommend `using Foo` for ephemeral work (e.g. REPL work).



","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42080/reactions","total_count":51,"+1":31,"-1":6,"laugh":0,"hooray":2,"confused":5,"heart":7,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42080/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1743452752","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080#issuecomment-1743452752","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42080","id":1743452752,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5n6vpQ","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-10-02T17:30:34Z","updated_at":"2023-10-02T17:30:34Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Seeing `using Foo` in packages has driven me crazy since I first saw it. I have experienced many of the concrete issues people mentioned above. I don't think I saw this mentioned, which happened a few weeks ago: Say I am trying to reduce the load time for my package. I find the culprit is the direct dependency `FooA`.  And 80 percent of the load time of `FooA` is, in turn, `FooB`. It looks to me like `FooB` could likely be an optional dependency, or it's effect could be otherwise mitigated (replace it or split `FooA`, or...). So where does `FooA` use `FooB`? I have no idea, because I only see `using FooB` at the top of `FooA.jl`.

On the other hand, this is really an issue of style, albeit an important one. Ideally, it does not belong on the main language documentation. I'm not sure, but I strongly suspect that nothing like this occurs in rust docs, and that this is intentional. Style is important there too, but it is in a separate document. However, I don't see anyone with the time or inclination to spend the (very large) effort required to separate these concerns in the Julia documentation.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1743452752/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51624","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51624/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51624/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51624/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51624","id":1930965531,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5zGDIb","number":51624,"title":"`lstrip` could be more efficient","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-10-06T21:42:05Z","updated_at":"2023-10-06T21:42:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"If I replace `pairs` here
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/6fc6a97d1dbc7b7c6172b26afb28e845c8888c8e/base/strings/util.jl#L363-L369

with enumerate, I find `lstrip` is almost twice as fast for some `String` and `SubString`.

This should probably be investigated more broadly. That is for other uses of `pairs` at least in `Base`. Maybe another method just for `String` and `Substring` (or a trait to be more thorough, but complicated)

Or try to fix whatever is slowing down `pairs`.

In retrospect (too late now) `pairs` could still call `keys` and `values`, but just construct `Tuple`s. Or was `struct Pair` really needed?
```julia
julia> a = lpad("March", 20);

julia> @btime lstrip2($a)  #  use `enumerate`
  24.901 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
"March"

julia> @btime lstrip($a)
  51.897 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
"March"
```

Versions v1.6, v1.9, v1.11","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51624/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51624/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

I replace `pairs` here
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/6fc6a97d1dbc7b7c6172b26afb28e845c8888c8e/base/strings/util.jl#L363-L369

with enumerate, I find `lstrip` is almost twice as fast for some `String` and `SubString`.

This should probably be investigated more broadly. That is for other uses of `pairs` at least in `Base`. Maybe another method just for `String` and `Substring` (or a trait to be more thorough, but complicated)

Or try to fix whatever is slowing down `pairs`.

In retrospect (too late now) `pairs` could still call `keys` and `values`, but just construct `Tuple`s. Or was `struct Pair` really needed?

### Example

```julia
julia> a = lpad("March", 20);

julia> @btime lstrip2($a)  #  use `enumerate`
  24.901 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
"March"

julia> @btime lstrip($a)
  51.897 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
"March"
```

### Version

```julia
function lstrip2(f, s::AbstractString)
    f === identity && isascii(s) && return lstrip(f, s)
    e = lastindex(s)
    for (i::Int, c::AbstractChar) in enumerate(s)
        !f(c) && return @inbounds SubString(s, i, e)
    end
    SubString(s, e+1, e)
end
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1751713548/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org/issues/1908","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org/issues/1908/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org/issues/1908/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org/issues/1908/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org/issues/1908","id":1756104723,"node_id":"I_kwDOBTjKgc5orAgT","number":1908,"title":"Blog post proposal: "Modern Julia workflows"","user":{"login":"gdalle","id":22795598,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyNzk1NTk4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22795598?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gdalle","html_url":"https://github.com/gdalle","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gdalle/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gdalle/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gdalle/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gdalle/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gdalle/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gdalle/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gdalle/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gdalle/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gdalle/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":24,"created_at":"2023-06-14T05:47:17Z","updated_at":"2023-06-16T16:15:49Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

AFAIK, the typical tools and packages a Julia user needs daily are not documented in a single place. 
* In the documentation, the section on "workflow tips" only mentions Revise and the REPL (<https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/workflow-tips/>)
* On the front page, the section "Essential tools" is very short and quite opinionated, with no descriptions (<https://julialang.org/>)

**Describe the solution you'd like**

A lengthy blog post detailing the typical workflow for using, developing and testing packages. See structure proposal below.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

* Adding things to the documentation (but where?)
* Redesign the front page (but how long would it take?)

**Additional context**

Related issues:
* https://github.com/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org/issues/800

Possible contributors:
* @jacobusmmsmit","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org/issues/1908/reactions","total_count":6,"+1":5,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org/issues/1908/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org/issues/comments/1594933135","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org/issues/1908#issuecomment-1594933135","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/www.julialang.org/issues/1908","id":1594933135,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBTjKgc5fEL-P","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-06-16T16:15:49Z","updated_at":"2023-06-16T16:15:49Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@gdalle I think you are saying that you want to separate the beginner-friendly things from the more advanced stuff. I think this is a great idea.  Maybe a beginner just wants a minimal recipe or two for being comfortable when trying to code a project in Julia. So `Pkg` and `Revise` (or the VSCode equivalent) are essential. Cthulu (I refuse to look up how to spell that correctly!) is not essential for beginners. It doesn't need to be reserved for advanced users, but at least one level beyond beginner. It's easy to be overwhelmed.

Also, I use JET in testing and CI. In other words, I would group it with Aqua. The interface is a bit rough, but it can catch regressions in design. You want to catch this before you have erected too much on top of the problematic code. On the other hand, I do this by copying other scripts and handrolling scripts to filter out what I want to consider a false positive. JET is not ready for use by beginners in this capacity. Yes. Often `itr` and `collect(itr)` are considered semantically equivalent. In fact, Base defines `collect(itr::Any)`, which tries to collect an iterator, in one case assuming the container created "implements" `push!`. Sometimes, in response to requests like this, you see. Well, you should do `findthingy(collect(itr), blah)`. 

It doesn't make sense to me to implement semantics for default keys in some places, but not here.

I don't have a clear view of when they are sort of the same. I used "Often" to indicate that I don't claim they are unconditionally equivalent. There are many places, all over the ecosystem, where passing an iterator fails, while passing `collect(itr)` succeeds, even thought the elements are just processed sequentially. Suppose such function were to accept either `itr` and `collect(itr)` and give the same result. The simplest case is if it just does `for x in a`.  Or, if we arrange for the keys of the former to be the same as the keys of the latter, there would be no semantic difference, in fact no detectable difference, inside the function.

I haven't seen a concrete example of problems that would arise from being able to do something like `pairs` or `keys`. But, I do understand that the consequences would have to be considered carefully. One objection is that perhaps some iterator naturally has keys other than `1, 2, ...`. An  answer is, define a method, or use a trait or something to give this iterator other keys. Maybe a method for `enumerate` or `pairs` or `keys`.

Another idea is an entirely new function `ienumerate` or `ipairs` that gives the behavior we want.

Hmmm. I'm confused about the current status. If I do `z = (x for x in 1:3)`, then `keys(z)` and `pairs(z)` do work with 1.7, but not 1.6.  At least for `Generator`s, the iterator is pulled out. I suppose this is uncontroversial, because these are they only keys that make sense in this case. What we are asking for is a way to give an iterator with no concept of keys the same keys that `collect` gives it.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1397084204/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34851","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34851/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34851/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34851/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34851","id":569536761,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU1Njk1MzY3NjE=","number":34851,"title":"Define pairs(A) = enumerate(A)","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2020-02-23T18:26:21Z","updated_at":"2023-01-31T15:28:12Z","closed_at":"2023-01-31T15:28:11Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The method for `findall` with no specialization on the collection type is
```julia
findall(testf::Function, A) = collect(first(p) for p in pairs(A) if testf(last(p)))
```

For a singly-linked list, like `DataStructures.Cons`, `findall` fails because there is no method `keys(::Cons)`. Constructing, say linear indices for `Cons` requires traversing the list to find it's length; more precisely, I don't see a way around traversal. Writing a fallback method

```julia
pairs(A) = enumerate(A)
```

would solve the problem for any iterable, including `Cons`. This assumes that
by default the keys for a collection are `1, 2, ...`.
In the case of `Cons`, it's possible that traversing twice, preallocating output after the first pass, would be more efficient. I did not try this, but I doubt it is more efficient.

See https://github.com/JuliaCollections/DataStructures.jl/issues/580 .

A somewhat related issue: I think the single argument method for `findall`, which is currently

```julia
findall(A) = collect(first(p) for p in pairs(A) if last(p))
```

would be better refactored as
```julia
findall(A) = findall(isequal(true), A)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34851/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34851/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1410585491","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34851#issuecomment-1410585491","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34851","id":1410585491,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5UE9OT","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-01-31T15:28:11Z","updated_at":"2023-01-31T15:28:11Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Yes. the [section on General Collections](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/collections/#General-Collections) it's not stated explicitly whether a general collection *must* implement all of `isempty`, `empty!`, `length`,  `checked_length`. Or *may* implement them? Or something else?

It's not clear what "Fully implemented by:" means in this section. The types listed in fact don't implement all of these. Immutable types (eg `Tuple`) typically do not implement `empty!`.  

Maybe I am reading the section incorrectly, but it seems to me that this should be clarified. [See this discourse post](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/why-is-there-no-abstract-collection-type/89233/12)

depot path for existing juliaup dir","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-11-30T08:54:02Z","updated_at":"2022-11-30T16:24:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/pulls/510","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup/pull/510","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup/pull/510.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup/pull/510.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Old behaviour would pick the first segment of JULIA_DEPOT_PATH to find the juliaup dir. This commit finds the first segment with a juliaup/juliaup.json file. If no such are found, it picks the first segment. Empty segments are expanded to the default path (which is ~/.julia/juliaup). If JULIA_DEPOT_PATH is not set, or it is set to the empty string, it will also use the default path.

__NOTE__: Julia docs on `JULIA_DEPOT_PATH` says that if the variable is set to the empty string, it means `DEPOT_PATH` is empty. It would be nice to be consistent with Base, and have it so an empty JULIA_DEPOT_PATH would resolve to no path instead of the default. However, this won't work for Juliaup, since it needs a directory to check for new versions. Hence, an empty variable just returns the default instead.

Note also that this approach does _not_ solve #257. If I understand that issue correctly, @jlapeyre wants Juliaup to search in the default depot path, even if that is not included in `JULIA_DEPOT_PATH`, such  that Julia can be forced to install _packages_ in a custom location, without forcing Juliaup to update to the same custom location. To solve this issue, we need to come up with a principled strategy for which directories Juliaup works in.

This might not solve #276. The user can set the env variable to `my_own_dir:systemwide_juliaup_dir`, and Juliaup will correctly pick the systemwide directory. However, the package manager will also pick that, if packages are not found in the self directory.

It might be necessary to have the Juliaup directory be separate from the package directory. Though we would need to think about exactly how. As I see it, there are two use cases:

* Common use case: User just wants to use their normal depot path both to load and install packages, and to use Juliaup and install new versions. This is what Juliaup already does.
* Uncommon use case: There is a systemwide `Juliaup` install, where individual users don't have (or should have) write permissions. Users want Juliaup to find versions in that location, but does not want Juliaup to begin writing to that location, since that would result in a permission error. Packages should be installed to a user-specific directory.

It seems to me a solution could be to have a specific env variable for Juliaup, independent of JULIA_DEPOT_PATH. Juliaup would then, if this variable is set, only look at that path. If it is not set, it would do as it does now.

Closes #323","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/510/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/510/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/comments/1332423319","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup/pull/510#issuecomment-1332423319","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/510","id":1332423319,"node_id":"IC_kwDOEE6KR85PayqX","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-11-30T16:24:11Z","updated_at":"2022-11-30T16:24:11Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"We need some solution to decoupling the depots from the julia installation. There are reasonable scenarios where you would want this, and juliaup does not support it at the moment.
> a solution could be to have a specific env variable for Juliaup

This one seems simple.

I think that this PR *does* fix #257 (there may be some unforeseen problem.) I think the statement of the problem could be more clear in that issue.

The trailing colon in the following is interpreted as an empty path, which is expanded to the default depot.
```shell
$ export JULIA_DEPOT_PATH="~/newdepot:"
$ julia -e "println(DEPOT_PATH)"
["/home/lapeyre/newdepot", "/home/lapeyre/.julia", 
 "/home/lapeyre/packages/julias/julia-1.8/local/share/julia",
 "/home/lapeyre/packages/julias/julia-1.8/share/julia"]
```
The current behavior of juliaup is to install julia in `newdepot`.

If I understand this PR correctly:  If `newdepot` has no juliaup-installed julia, juliaup will next look in the default depot and find the exisiting installation.

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/comments/1332423319/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

If JULIA_DEPOT_PATH is not set, or it is set to the empty string, it will also use the default path.

It would be nice to be consistent with Base, and have it so an empty JULIA_DEPOT_PATH would resolve to no path instead of the default. This might not solve #276. The user can set the env variable to `my_own_dir:systemwide_juliaup_dir`, and Juliaup will correctly pick the systemwide directory. However, the package manager will also pick that, if packages are not found in the self directory.

* Uncommon use case: There is a systemwide `Juliaup` install, where individual users don't have (or should have) write permissions. Users want Juliaup to find versions in that location, but does not want Juliaup to begin writing to that location, since that would result in a permission error. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47005","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47005/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47005/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47005/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47005","id":1393591637,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5TEIVV","number":47005,"title":"Predicate version of allequal","user":{"login":"GregPlowman","id":8840717,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg4NDA3MTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8840717?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/GregPlowman","html_url":"https://github.com/GregPlowman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/GregPlowman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/GregPlowman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/GregPlowman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/GregPlowman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/GregPlowman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/GregPlowman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/GregPlowman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/GregPlowman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/GregPlowman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-10-01T23:53:57Z","updated_at":"2022-10-12T16:59:43Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Like some other functions, e.g. `all`, `findall`, I sometimes want the predicate version of allequal:

So I'll post it here.

* There are some efficiency gains, eg using `peel` that would help even with the current implementation
* I also added an argument that specifies returning the common value. I'm thinking of common tasks similar to `LinearAlgebra.check_square`. This would take some thought to do in a uniform way across Julia. Some things like this are in the ecosystme. Really, what would be convenient is to get the common string length an array of strings and throw an error if they differ.
* The methods with `Val{true}` might need to use `Some` or something like that.

```julia
# peel is significantly faster for short itr and early exit from all
function Base.allequal(func, itr)
    isempty(itr) && return true
    itr1, itr_rest = Iterators.peel(itr)
    val = func(itr1) # faster if we lift this from loop, even without peel
    return all(x -> isequal(func(x), val), itr_rest)
end

# Return the common value if all are equal, otherwise false
function Base.allequal(itr, ::Val{true})
    isempty(itr) && return nothing
    itr1, itr_rest = Iterators.peel(itr)
    return all(x -> isequal(x, itr1), itr_rest) ? itr1 : false
end

function Base.allequal(func, itr, ::Val{true})
    isempty(itr) && return nothing
    itr1, itr_rest = Iterators.peel(itr)
    val = func(itr1)
    return all(x -> isequal(func(x), val), itr_rest) ? val : false
end

Base.allequal(func, itr, ::Val{false}) = allequal(func, itr)
```

I think this is very common
```julia
common_property = allequal_throw(some_property, itr)  # throw error if not all equal,
```
Maybe we need to allow passing a function to call that throws.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1276477572/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/PackageCompiler.jl/issues/716","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/PackageCompiler.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/PackageCompiler.jl/issues/716/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/PackageCompiler.jl/issues/716/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/PackageCompiler.jl/issues/716/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/PackageCompiler.jl/issues/716","id":1345542085,"node_id":"I_kwDOBplwkM5QM1fF","number":716,"title":"Split `run_compiler` so users can get the location of the compiler?","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-08-21T17:05:06Z","updated_at":"2022-08-21T17:05:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I want to use `PackageCompiler` for a completely different project that involves compiling C code. It would be useful if I could just find the compiler. You could rewrite `run_compiler` like this:
```julia
function run_compiler(cmd::Cmd; cplusplus::Bool=false)
    compiler_cmd = find_compiler_cmd(cplusplus)
    full_cmd = `$compiler_cmd $cmd`
    @debug "running $full_cmd"
    run(full_cmd) 
end
```
And put the rest of the existing function body in `find_compiler_cmd` instead. Then we could call `find_compiler_cmd` for other projects.

As it is, we can still just call `run_compiler`. But, if the function were split as suggested here, we'd have more flexibility, and options for debugging and handling exceptional situations.
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/PackageCompiler.jl/issues/716/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/PackageCompiler.jl/issues/716/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

For example:
```julia
julia> using IsApprox: Approx, isinteger
julia> isinteger(1 + 1e-5, Approx())
false

julia> isinteger(10000 + 1e-5, Approx())
true
```

The motivation for `IsApprox` is to think about a uniform way of treating `isanumericalthing(...)` for all different kinds of things and all notions of "approximate". I found that this is treated piecemeal in different ways in different packages.

Here are is the [code for `IsApprox.isinteger`](https://github.com/jlapeyre/IsApprox.jl/blob/d53bb3de69da2d65c61327cec130e1e030566aa9/src/base_applications.jl#L104-L125)

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45152","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45152/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45152/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45152/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45152","id":1223014445,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5I5bgt","number":45152,"title":"add method for foreach that takes no function","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-05-02T15:18:05Z","updated_at":"2022-05-02T15:18:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"People (in general) are familiar and comfortable with comprehensions. For example `[f(k, v) for (k, v) in dict]`.
But, if I only want to call `f(k, v)` for side effects, using a comprehension is slow/wasteful. It would be nice if there were a way to write something as close as possible to a comprehension, but that does not store results.

I can do this.
```julia
for (k, v) in dict
    f(k, v)
end
```
Or this `foreach(((k,v),) -> f(k, v), dict)`.  The latter is OK, but I avoid it; for example when sharing code with people new to Julia. (although using a trailing comma to mean "create a tuple" is also known in Python.)

So far, the best solution I see is this
```julia
foreach(f(k, v) for (k, v) in dict)
```
This would work with the following method.
```julia
Base.foreach(g::Base.Generator) = foreach(identity, g)
```

Arguments in favor: 
* It is clear immediately to any Julia user and many newcomers what `foreach(f(k, v) for (k, v) in dict)` does. (*Maybe* a few would expect this to be equivalent to a comprehension)
* It adds no verbs or nouns to Base
* It is a natural, unsurprising, extension to `foreach`. It fits very well with `foreach`'s mission.
* It is very easy to read and understand the new code in `Base`. It adds very little complexity.
* There are no other contenders for what `foreach(::Generator)` should mean.


Some people don't like `foreach`. There are reasonable arguments against it. So if your goal is to move towards deprecation and eventually removing `foreach` (maybe in v2) then extending its use would not be attractive.

For me, it's not `foreach` that is so attractive iteself. I'm just looking for a way to write it that is as close to comprehension/generator syntax as possible. This means how can you most simply iterate over a generator while discarding values ?

Some people don't like `foreach`. There are reasonable arguments against it. So if your goal is to move towards deprecation and eventually removing `foreach` (maybe in v2) then extending its use would not be attractive.

For me, it's not `foreach` that is so attractive iteself. I'm just looking for a way to write it that is as close to comprehension/generator syntax as possible. This means how can you most simply iterate over a generator while discarding values ?

It has this method: `foreach(f) = (f(); nothing)`. This method is precisely for the case you want. So doing something else for things that aren't callable at least reclaims some space. There appears to be no `foreach` call with a single argument in base and stdlib. On the other hand, if you want `foreach(f)` to curry  in v2, then this is perfect, you won't break any code.

I kinda like `discard`, after this comment: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/19198#issuecomment-386495870.

```julia
discard(v[i] = w[i] + 1 for i in eachindex(w))
```

If you are thinking about the argument as an iterable, then it's pretty clear what this does. But, if you are a casual user, you might think, *"it does that thing I want just as I wrote, but they told me I have to write 'discard', too. What's that about? Does it throw away `w`?"* I mean, in this case the line is clearly intended for its side effect, so the computer must understand what I want. Another possibility is `consume`, but it has the same problem. (or how about `eat`, it's suitably short)

`iterateall` might strike a good balance between signaling intent to the programmer/developer and the casual user.

Another possibility is `run(v[i] = w[i] + 1 for i in eachindex(w))`. You could say that this is too far from the meaning of `run(::Cmd)`. But, BenchmarkTools also uses `run` for something else, and alot of people have it imported all the time.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1115438497/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45323","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45323/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45323/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45323/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45323","id":1237272988,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5Jv0mc","number":45323,"title":"Error in documentation on macro hygiene","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-05-16T14:44:59Z","updated_at":"2022-05-16T14:44:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The documentation on macro hygiene says explicitly that with this macro
```julia
macro mytime1(ex)
    return quote
        local t0 = time_ns()
        local val = $ex
        local t1 = time_ns()
        println("elapsed time: ", (t1-t0)/1e9, " seconds")
        val
    end
end
```
the code `@mytime1 t0 = 0` will work as intended.
But, it doesn't:
```julia
julia> @mytime1 (t0 = 0);
elapsed time: 4.576732909746654e6 seconds
```
However, writing  `local val = $(esc(ex))` *does* make the example work as intended:
```julia
macro mytime2(ex)
    return quote
        local t0 = time_ns()
        local val = $(esc(ex))
        local t1 = time_ns()
        println("elapsed time: ", (t1-t0)/1e9, " seconds")
        val
    end
end

julia> @mytime2 (t0 = 0);
elapsed time: 7.52e-7 seconds
```

The documentation goes on to say that doing `$(esc(ex))`, as in `@mytime2`, is indeed necessary for a robust macro; but for other reasons. To emphasize again, it explicitly says that " assignment[s] to a variable called `t0`" does not require `esc`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45323/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45323/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}

If I understand your comment correctly, it's worse than that. It essentially says:

Here is a macro with no `esc` that introduces a variable `t0` in the body. But what if the expression  passed by the caller also assigns to a variable called `t0`?  How will they be made distinct? No worry; the rules when to gensym a variable already prevent this from happening, *without* needing to use `esc`.

But, in fact, the `esc` *is* necessary in this particular case.

2. State that there are two potential problems prob 1) user input `ex` assigns to a "variable called `t0`". prob 2) "defined its own time_ns variable."
3. State that the macro expander solves the problem in the following way:
4. Give the rules for when to apply hygiene to parts of the expression. I interpret this as the entire "way" referred to in 3. There is a "First" and a "...then". So two parts. You might want to say that the text several lines below about `esc` are also part of the "way". But the next item, number 5, makes it clear that there is no more to the "way", we are done. (of course later we find that the "way" is not really enough. But, is enough for the two problems at hand.)
5. Declare that this solves the two problems: "both of the above concerns are handled".  prob 1) " macro's locals will not conflict with any user variables" and prob 2) "time_ns and println will refer to the Julia Base definitions" [I added the numbers here as the my interpretation of what refers to what]
6. State that "one" problem remains. You might think that this means either one of the two problems remain, or that there is a third heretofore unconsidered problem. And "one problem remains" seems to imply the first meaning, not the second. Maybe. But, based on all the text above, I interpret this to mean we consider now a third problem. 
7.  Give an example of the "remaining" problem. I think the following agrees with the rules for when to rename variables. Note that the following is with *no* escape. Because the discussion in the doc is premised on no `esc` yet.
```julia
julia> striplines(macroexpand(Main, :(@mytime1 t0=1)))
quote
    local var"#139#t0" = Main.time_ns()
    local var"#138#val" = (var"#139#t0" = 1)
    local var"#140#t1" = Main.time_ns()
    Main.println("elapsed time: ", (var"#140#t1" - var"#139#t0") / 1.0e9, " seconds")
    var"#138#val"
end

julia> striplines(macroexpand(Main, :(@mytime1 global t0=1)))
quote
    local Main.t0 = Main.time_ns()
    local var"#141#val" = global t0 = 1
    local var"#142#t1" = Main.time_ns()
    Main.println("elapsed time: ", (var"#142#t1" - Main.t0) / 1.0e9, " seconds")
    var"#141#val"
end
```
But, they don't prevent the "user" variables and the macros variables from colliding. It's even kinda clear for why both `t0`s are renamed to the same thing. The doc refers to the "variables within a macro result", which I interpret to mean regardless of where they came from.

I suppose the entire problem and solution up to this point has been about protecting the variables defined by the macro. And I agree, they have been solved.

Then the third thing, solved by `esc` is preventing the things you don't want from being renamed. If this is what the doc means, then yes, I suppose it's unclear rather than wrong. I mean, the text up to the point of "one problem remains" should make it clear that if I pass a `t0` in my expression it is going to collide with `t0` in the macro.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1127990294/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I don't have a good grasp on different practical workflows that involve manipulating `DEPOT_PATH`.  Checking a few scenarios: If I do "export JULIA_DEPOT_PATH=~/newdepot:", then this depot is prepended the usual contents of `DEPOT_PATH`. If `~/newdepot` is empty, then the current behavior is `~/.juliaup/bin/julia` begins to download a julia installation. If I interrupt this, and start julia again. Then julialauncher sees `~/newdepot/juliaup` with no config file in it and says: "The Julia launcher failed to figure out ...".  In this case, searching through `DEPOT_PATH` would have found the juliaup installs.  So, it's useful if people do this.

 (Here is the the  [doc for `DEPOT_PATH`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/constants/#Base.DEPOT_PATH)  and [for `JULIA_DEPOT_PATH`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/environment-variables/#JULIA_DEPOT_PATH).)


But, I am doing something different. What I am trying to do is *replace* the first entry in `DEPOT_PATH`, because I want to force new packages to be installed there (I have a reason that I omit for brevity). If I leave `~/.julia` in `DEPOT_PATH`, then packages will be found there. I wouldn't be surprised if there are other use cases for this.

If you want to support `julialauncher` working in this case, then you'd want to search in the default depot even if it's not in `DEPOT_PATH`.   The file `initdefs.jl` has exactly this: `joinpath(homedir(), ".julia")`

I have a library that looks for julia installations, juliaup, jill, the user's PATH, etc. It takes the version that best matches a spec. For now, I simply remove `~/.juliaup/bin/julia` if it appears (eg on the user's PATH). The juliaup installs will usually be found anyway. So for my immediate use case, I am able to work around the issue.... There was another problem due to the symlink to the launcher encountered when scripting. If I can recall, I can open an issue.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/comments/1059839827/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

What I am trying to do is read `juliaup api getconfig1`. That fails if `juliaup` is, say, downloading a julia. Is it possible/sensible to have only a write lock? {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/242","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/242/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/242/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/242/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/242","id":1124424729,"node_id":"I_kwDOEE6KR85DBVwZ","number":242,"title":"feature: include installation paths in API","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-02-04T17:07:50Z","updated_at":"2022-02-04T17:07:50Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The installation paths are currently not in the API. I saw somewhere that @davidanthoff had done this intentionally, but had not made a firm decision. I find complicated, un-documented installation trees unpleasantly enterpris-y, but I am in a small minority. There are big advantages to hiding the paths, especially in the early stages of development. I'm asking what the status is and I give a use case below. But, I understand that the pros of hiding this detail may outweigh cons.

I have a specific use case. I am interested in trying to package python projects that depend on Julia in such a way that the user does not have to know that Julia is involved; it's just `pip install ...` like any other Python package. In many environments, this is essential for adopting Julia (until Julia rules the world and calls the shots). For example Python packages with compiled C or rust shared libs can be distributed this way. This was a necessary condition, for example for adopting a [rust](https://github.com/Qiskit/retworkx) library in the IBM Qiskit ecosystem.  This library is a successful open-source project that has industry support. Doing this right now for Julia is perhaps a bit quixotic, but I suspect it is in fact not too quixotic. I am developing a (rather simple) package [julia_project](https://github.com/jlapeyre/julia_project) that manages a Julia dependency in a Python project. It depends on another package [find_julia](https://github.com/jlapeyre/find_julia) whose sole responsibility is search for Julia installations (with some support for coarse version requirements) on a system, and return a path, or offer to install Julia if none is found.

I'd like to include juliaup installation paths in `find_julia`. If the user chose to put the julia executable parent dir in their PATH, then `find_julia` will find and use it (this is `~/.juliaup/bin/julia` in Linux, I suppose). So, including the path in the juliaup API is probably only useful to the extent that the link to the executable or launcher is *not* in their PATH. I don't know how often that is. My `julia` is sometimes a script that calls julia in a particular way. A package author might use `find_julia` in such a way as to avoid picking up this script.

A related question is, is there a plan for a programmatic, or at least more automated, interface for installation via juliaup? `find_julia` currently uses `jill.py`, which includes both a cli and library interface. It is very easy to use and requires almost no work on the part of `find_julia`. A crucial ingredient is that the installation paths are part of the jill.py API. In addition, I have a (not quite finished) PR to jill.py that searches for and returns the path. This would work even if the API were not published. Using juliaup in `find_julia` is currently not an option because I would have to do a lot of detective work and download the installer of the installer for the correct platform, etc. Downloading a single, cross-platform script, that would perform the installation, perhaps with environment variable control, would make juliaup a viable option. The big impediment to making jill.py universal is that mechanisms for bundling a python interpreter seem to be poor to non-existent at the moment.

I use fish and try to only change my path in the bash startup files and then import them into fish. But, some tools detect that I am using fish and add a path somewhere else, etc.

The package depends on https://github.com/jlapeyre/julia_project, which factors out managing a Julia project within a python package.

The latter depends in turn on https://github.com/jlapeyre/find_julia, which handles finding a path to a Julia exectuable, and if not found installing Julia via jill.py.

The directory with the system-image-compilation scripts is here:
 https://github.com/jlapeyre/diffeq_julia/tree/main/diffeq_julia/sys_image

`compile_exercise_script.jl` is currently a dummy example. If it were replaced by something to exercise the Julia differential equations packages, then latency would be reduced.

But, you could not use both diffeqpy and quickpomdps together in one python runtime. `julia_project` should be modified to accomodate this. I'm not sure how to do it.

I think you *can* use these two projects together as they are, without `julia_project`. But, any messiness, conflicting libpython (which will happen *often* under windows IIUC) , creating a new Julia project, etc. would have to be handled manually. One goal of `julia_project` is to insulate the Python user from Julia (at least they have the choice to ignore Julia). Python modules written in Rust don't require the Python user to touch rust in any way.  I see this as a way to drive Julia adoption.

I should probably fork and modify quickpomdps just so I can experiment with ways to get the two projects to work together. One clue might be in something David Anthoff wrote: The Project.toml  (and Manifest.toml) serves two purposes, to define packages and to define environments. The uses are separate and the file `Project.toml` could (should) have been given two different names. I think in `julia_project` it's not clear which role it is playing. We might want to manage two Project.tomls. One for each Python package, eg diffeqpy. This is like a package Project.toml. It can include a compat section. But `julia_project` would also manage a Python module-level Project.toml that represents the environment. `pyjulia` already stores things at the module level, i.e. managing Julia is not all encapsulated in a class. This is more complicated than I like, but we may be forced into something like this.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/diffeqpy/issues/comments/1010244420/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

+import julia
+import logging
+
+from julia_project import JuliaProject
+
+import os
+diffeqpy_path = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
+
+julia_project = JuliaProject(
+    name="diffeqpy",
+    package_path=diffeqpy_path,
+    preferred_julia_versions = ['1.7', '1.6', 'latest'],
+    env_prefix = 'DIFFEQPY_',
+    logging_level = logging.INFO, # or logging.WARN,
+    console_logging=False
+)
+

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/diffeqpy/pulls/comments/782588003","pull_request_review_id":849815086,"id":782588003,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOB8dM5M4upVhj","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,38 @@
+import julia
+import logging
+
+from julia_project import JuliaProject
+
+import os
+diffeqpy_path = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
+
+julia_project = JuliaProject(
+    name="diffeqpy",
+    package_path=diffeqpy_path,
+    preferred_julia_versions = ['1.7', '1.6', 'latest'],
+    env_prefix = 'DIFFEQPY_',
+    logging_level = logging.INFO, # or logging.WARN,
+    console_logging=False
+)","path":"diffeqpy/_julia_project.py","position":16,"original_position":16,"commit_id":"32d1e376663dd54ac1927b181c54b8fe3536e2af","original_commit_id":"32d1e376663dd54ac1927b181c54b8fe3536e2af","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I understand the gist, but maybe not precisely what you are asking. Yes, `JuliaProject` does binary discovery and installation via another python package, `find_julia`, which uses jill.py. That part could be separated out, and the much of the rest could be done in Julia. I considered this. One argument for using Python is that errors and stack traces are more likely to be in Python this way. And a Python user is more likely to be able to spot an error, or make a suggestion or improvement. I'm trying, to the extent possible, to hide Julia from the end user. Admittedly, in diffeqpy, the interface to Julia is rather low level. But in other python projects, Julia is better hidden behind a Python API. I often work in environments where 0% of the people have enough interest in Julia to ever try it for anything. And 100% of the people love Python. Something like `julia_project` is a good way to introduce the possibility of Julia. On the other hand, I'll think more about whether more of it might be better written in > I think there should be a language-agnostic way to handle julia installations with a single transparent user interface. It'd be bad if each language/framework handles Julia installations in its own way.

Here are several thoughts on choosing an installer. The bottom line is that using juliaup would be much more difficult for my purposes, which is to make installing a python module that depends on Julia as easy as installing a python module that depends on a rust or c++ library.

* I agree it would be best to have a single interface. But, I agree with the comments in [JuliaPy/pyjulia/#473](https://github.com/JuliaPy/pyjulia/issues/473) in that it is premature to pick a winner.

* jill.py is a library, but also a cross-platform command line application. It's not Python centric. It's an application written in Python.

* Python is a great language for writing an installer. juliaup is written in Rust, a high-performance systems language. It is much harder to develop, and has a much smaller base of possible contributors and eyeballs. This is worth it if you need high performance. But, that's not the case here.

* When writing juliaup David Anthoff went C++ -> Julia -> Rust. I'm not sure why, except that clearly the last choice is the best of the three. It could well be that it is easier to deploy a self-contained rust program than a python program. I don't really know. But that would be a great argument for using rust over python. There are probably other good reasons to choose rust that I don't know about. But, the advantages of jill.py are transparent to me.

* The location of Julia installations, and links to them is clearly documented for all platforms in jill.py. It is not documented at all for juliaup. 

* juliaup is more complicated than jill.py and the organization of the installed files and auxiliary binaries is a bit more complicated. The whole thing includes shell scripts, windows cmd scripts, xml files, rust source and compiled binary.  There may be some benefit to the complexity.

* juliaup for osx and linux is labeled experimental with known bugs. (In practice, it worked for the basic functionality on my  system)

* I am looking for something that allows a Python user to do `pip install thepackage`, and then `import thepackage`.  So, I would have to detect which platform is being used and then download the appropriate juliaup installer (there are seven of them) (unless it's already installed) and then run the installer.

* David Anthoff currently wants to hide the installation location of julia as an implementation detail and automatically add it to the user's path. This is a reasonable choice. But, it conflicts with other choices. For example, I like to  make `julia` a script that runs julia with a particular system image.  Johnny Chen already  agreed to make returning a list of installed versions and their paths part of the jill.py library API (I did not finish the PR yet, but I copied the code to find_julia). Looking at my juliaup installation, I see that getting this information would be more complicated.

* jill.py is both a library and an application. juliaup is only an application. So, find_julia and julia_project can be optionally controlled by environment variables, rather than interactive questions. This makes using containers, and test frameworks easier. As far as I can tell, this is not possible with juliaup.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/diffeqpy/issues/comments/1010576804/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

PR uses julia_project and find_julia to handle installing and managing Julia and Julia packages.
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/diffeqpy/issues/100/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/diffeqpy/issues/100/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/diffeqpy/issues/comments/1011633016","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/diffeqpy/pull/100#issuecomment-1011633016","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/diffeqpy/issues/100","id":1011633016,"node_id":"IC_kwDOB8dM5M48TEt4","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-01-13T01:24:39Z","updated_at":"2022-01-13T01:24:39Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> Why not install juliaup on the fly and then use it to install Julia? The point is that the user has access to the application storage from a CLI and it's shared across all languages and frameworks.

As I said above, jill.py is a cross-platform application. Its a CLI application
```
shell> jill --help | cat
INFO: Showing help with the command 'jill -- --help'.

NAME
    jill

SYNOPSIS
    jill COMMAND

COMMANDS
    COMMAND is one of the following:

     download
       download julia release from nearest servers

     install
       Install the Julia programming language for your current system

     upstream
       print all registered upstream servers

     mirror
       Download/sync all Julia releases

     list
       List all Julia executable versions in symlink dir

     switch
       Switch the julia target version or path.
```

If someone already has python installed or is willing to install it, then jill.py is clearly a far easier solution than juliaup. @sibyjackgrove  tested `julia_project` on windows even though I never tested it on windows, and it works. I could not do that with juliaup. (There were some install problems with `julia_project` in that case, but not due to windows, but rather cross-platform install issues that I corrected.)   For `julia_project` the user always has Python installed already, so jill.py is a clear winner. I wanted to have `find_julia` look for julia where it is installed by juliaup. But, unlike jill.py that's not documented. I could probably even use jill.py to install to the juliaup locations if I knew where they are. But, that would require me to test on several platforms or try to find someone to do it for me. Some or all  of the installation path is meant to be hidden from the API.    On linux, I can install several versions to find how the links in `~/.juliaup/bin` are done, and hope that that part is stable. (the links point to a tree inside `~/.julia`. If I knew what I could count on for all platforms, I would strongly consider being at least compatible with juliaup.

>  disagree that it's a good language for creating simple-to-distribute self-contained CLI. Of course, there are various ways to create a self-contained Python application but it's not as straightforward as using a language with an AOT compiler.

I strongly suspected this from the beginning. Why else would someone use an AOT compiler for this? I looked briefly yesterday for how to package a simple-to-distribute self-contained python application. I think I saw dead projects, old ill-maintained projects. It did not look encouraging at all. So yeah, using jill.py for people who don't want, or can't, install python would be tough.

Python rules the world. In the spaces I am targeting, nothing else matters. I have to be practical given my environment and very scare resources (mainly time). They have no incentive to accommodate us. I have to accommodate them. I want to maximize the probability that the Python world accepts things like this. The more Python they see, the happier they are. (Of course, there is a small minority that has a broader view). I also have to do all of this myself, including the project that I originally wanted to do. If I get time in the future to try to support juliaup, I think it would be a good idea. By the way `find_julia` does the searching and downloading, and `julia_project` depends only on the `find_julia` API. So, juliaup could be used in `find_julia` in the future. I have every incentive to try to support juliaup (I could also support the shell jill). If I can avoid downloading Julia, so much the better.

> All that said, let me note again that I'm not working on this and I have no intention to be a blocker.

Well you have by far the most experience in designing things to call Julia from Python. So, it is very useful to hear your opinions.  For instance, not doing work with side-effects when importing. So thanks for taking the time to weigh in!  (By the way, can you explain a bit more the situations in which side-effects on import are a problem?)

> Chris seems to like how things are handled in R which is similar to what is suggested in this PR, IIUC.

Oh, I need to check that out.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/diffeqpy/pulls/comments/784424168","pull_request_review_id":852378860,"id":784424168,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOB8dM5M4uwVzo","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,38 @@
+import julia
+import logging
+
+from julia_project import JuliaProject
+
+import os
+diffeqpy_path = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
+
+julia_project = JuliaProject(
+    name="diffeqpy",
+    package_path=diffeqpy_path,
+    preferred_julia_versions = ['1.7', '1.6', 'latest'],
+    env_prefix = 'DIFFEQPY_',
+    logging_level = logging.INFO, # or logging.WARN,
+    console_logging=False
+)
+
+julia_project.run()","path":"diffeqpy/_julia_project.py","position":18,"original_position":18,"commit_id":"32d1e376663dd54ac1927b181c54b8fe3536e2af","original_commit_id":"32d1e376663dd54ac1927b181c54b8fe3536e2af","user":{"login":"jlapeyre","id":1969884,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5Njk4ODQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1969884?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre","html_url":"https://github.com/jlapeyre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jlapeyre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"> I also consider that it's a language-agnostic best practice to avoid "magic" initialization

Yes, the  idea of `import package` reading a bunch of state from the filesystem plus implicit inputs (environment variables) and then changing a bunch of state seems like its abusing the import system. I can't see an actual serious problem, but it doesn't feel right. I really like the idea of the user not being required to remember to do anything to use the package. But, requiring `setup` or `install` the first time is perhaps not so bad.

>  it'd be impossible to use updated julia and DifferentialEquations

You could require that the user restart after upgrading. I think people are pretty used to software that tells them they have to do that.

> Do you generate a sysimage for each libpython?

If there is a conflicting `libpython` then `julia_project` gives the user the option of either rebuilding PyCall (and breaking whatever built it previously) or using a depot particular to the project and building a new PyCall (and all other packages) there. (I don't know of a more fine-grained option at the moment) In the tox test, so far, I only choose the latter. But, I think it may not be that. I did not spend much time debugging tox so
